refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) matching customer deployment scenarios. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation link (operator console → vehicle → operator console). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test runs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate streaming with forward error correction (FEC) and automatic codec switching based on network quality metrics.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (primary + backup) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-C: Use dedicated, quality-of-service (QoS) prioritized network infrastructure (private LTE or licensed spectrum) to guarantee packet loss performance.
DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining protocol optimization (UDP with selective retransmission), FEC, and network path diversity with fallback to lower-bandwidth modes.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining protocol optimization (UDP with selective retransmission), FEC, and network path diversity with fallback to lower-bandwidth modes. This approach balances cost, flexibility, and robustness across diverse customer network environments (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) while maintaining safety criticality.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation protocol logic to network infrastructure selection (DP for FR-NGHE-009.2), but this is acceptable because network conditions are a known constraint.
DP-D couples to real-time monitoring (FR-NGHE-009.3), requiring tight integration with telemetry and safety alert systems.
DP-D does not create circular dependencies; it is a solution concept that can be independently validated.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable through clear interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and modular codec/FEC plugin architecture. No axiomatic violation detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer network conditions are well-characterized and stable during operation. Risk: Unexpected network degradation (e.g., interference, congestion) could exceed packet loss threshold; mitigation: real-time monitoring and operator alerts.

Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth increase) is acceptable within customer bandwidth budget. Risk: FEC may consume excessive bandwidth on constrained links; mitigation: adaptive FEC tuning based on network state.

Assumption: Fallback to lower-bandwidth modes is operationally acceptable (reduced video quality, slower command response). Risk: Operator may reject degraded mode; mitigation: clear operational procedures and training.

Assumption: Redundant paths (dual network interfaces) are available on vehicle and operator console. Risk: Single-interface deployments cannot leverage path diversity; mitigation: graceful degradation to single-path mode.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define fallback hierarchy: (1) Adaptive FEC + codec switching (primary), (2) Path diversity failover (secondary), (3) Reduced-bandwidth mode (tertiary). Add acceptance criterion: system must achieve ≤1% packet loss in at least one fallback mode even if primary mode fails.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote equipment operation, including protocol selection, codec management, FEC implementation, network path management, and real-time quality monitoring. Scope includes operator-side and vehicle-side teleoperation stacks but excludes physical network infrastructure (which is customer-provided) and vehicle control logic (owned by Vehicle module).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle sensor telemetry (video, position, diagnostics) and sends operator commands (steering, throttle, bucket control). Telemetry interface carries both command and feedback streams; packet loss metrics are embedded in telemetry metadata.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives high-level task directives from Site Manager or autonomous planning layer (e.g., 'move to location X', 'perform excavation'). Tasking is translated into low-level teleoperation commands.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits safety alerts when packet loss exceeds threshold, communication latency becomes unsafe, or network conditions degrade. Safety Manager and Operator receive these alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with the physical network (WiFi, cellular, hybrid). Network Link provides raw packet transmission/reception; Teleoperation applies FEC, codec selection, and path management on top.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse teleoperation logic across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), violating cohesion. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle module owns physical equipment control (propulsion, hydraulics, sensors). Teleoperation protocol logic is orthogonal to vehicle mechanics. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would couple communication concerns with mechanical control, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation communication. DP-D (protocol optimization, FEC, path diversity, monitoring) is entirely within scope. High cohesion: all sub-components (codec manager, FEC engine, network monitor, failover logic) are tightly coupled and owned by a single team. Minimal diffusion risk. Strongly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alert generation. While SafetyAlert is an output of DP-D, the core communication logic (FEC, codec, path management) is not a safety service concern. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would create a false ownership boundary. Not recommended as primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP-D because: (1) all sub-components (protocol stack, FEC, codec manager, network monitor, failover logic) are functionally cohesive and serve a single purpose (reliable remote operation), (2) the module has clear external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) that decouple it from Vehicle and Safety Services, (3) ownership is unambiguous—a dedicated teleoperation team can design, test, and maintain the module independently, (4) diffusion risk is minimal; no other module needs to replicate or share teleoperation logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a 'god module' if additional communication concerns (e.g., firmware updates, diagnostics streaming) are added without clear boundaries. Mitigation: define strict interface contracts and resist scope creep.

Risk: Network Link interface is customer-provided and may have unpredictable characteristics (latency jitter, packet reordering). Teleoperation module must be robust to these variations. Mitigation: extensive testing under diverse network conditions; adaptive algorithms.

Risk: Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Vehicle (via Telemetry) could create circular dependencies if Vehicle module also implements communication logic. Mitigation: Vehicle module must be communication-agnostic; all protocol logic resides in Teleoperation.
Risk: SafetyAlert output must be reliable even if Teleoperation module is degraded. Mitigation: SafetyAlert generation must be fail-safe and independent of nominal teleoperation performance.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle: Teleoperation owns the communication pipeline (protocol, FEC, codec, network management); Vehicle owns command execution and sensor acquisition. Define a clear Telemetry interface contract specifying packet structure, latency bounds, and loss tolerance. Establish a separate Safety Alert channel that is independent of the main teleoperation data path to ensure safety criticality is not compromised by communication failures.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid protocol optimization, FEC, path diversity, fallback modes) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1% under agreed conditions). The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific technologies), scalable (adapts to diverse network environments), and verifiable (packet loss is measurable). No independence violation: DP-D is a single, coherent solution concept that does not require multiple independent DPs to satisfy the FR.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: Teleoperation module is a natural, cohesive owner. No duplication of DP-D logic across other modules is anticipated.
Moderate integration risk: Teleoperation module must interface cleanly with Vehicle (Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link. Interface contracts must be explicit and tested.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. All sub-components of DP-D (codec manager, FEC engine, network monitor, failover logic) fit naturally within the module boundary. Ownership is clear and unambiguous. No architectural packaging issues detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible within Teleoperation module scope. Packet loss can be measured end-to-end across the Telemetry interface. Real-time monitoring (FR-NGHE-009.3) is owned by Teleoperation. Acceptance testing can be conducted in isolation (with mock Vehicle and Network Link) or integrated with full system.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≤1% packet loss. Vehicle module is responsible for reliable command execution (given valid teleoperation input). Safety Services is responsible for alerting when packet loss exceeds threshold. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine the Telemetry interface contract to specify: (1) packet structure and serialization format, (2) latency bounds (e.g., command-to-execution latency ≤200ms), (3) loss tolerance per packet type (e.g., video loss is tolerable; critical commands must be retransmitted), (4) metadata for packet loss metrics.
Define a separate, fail-safe SafetyAlert channel that operates independently of the main teleoperation data path. This ensures safety criticality is not compromised if the main channel degrades.

Establish acceptance criteria for each fallback mode: (1) primary mode (adaptive FEC + codec switching) must achieve ≤1% loss, (2) secondary mode (path diversity failover) must achieve ≤2% loss, (3) tertiary mode (reduced-bandwidth) must achieve ≤5% loss. Operator must be able to select fallback mode manually if automatic failover is undesirable.

Add a requirement for real-time packet loss reporting to the operator and Site Manager (FR-NGHE-009.3 sub-FR). Teleoperation module must expose a monitoring interface (e.g., REST API or telemetry stream) that reports packet loss, latency, codec selection, and network path status.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (error detection/recovery, protocol/codec selection, real-time monitoring). DP-D is a coherent solution concept that addresses all three sub-FRs, but each sub-FR requires further refinement and design detail. Zigzag down to the next level to decompose each sub-FR into its own FR-DP-IM chain, ensuring that error detection, protocol selection, and monitoring are designed with equal rigor. This will also clarify ownership boundaries within the Teleoperation module (e.g., separate teams for codec management, FEC, and monitoring).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution. [Decompose into: selective retransmission, packet reordering, command deduplication]

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions. [Decompose into: codec selection algorithm, FEC tuning, protocol optimization]

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety manager. [Decompose into: packet loss measurement, alert generation, dashboard/API exposure]

revise.why: DP-D is a high-level solution concept that combines multiple techniques (FEC, codec switching, path diversity, fallback modes). At the next decomposition level, each sub-FR will require a more specific DP that details the algorithm, parameters, and implementation approach. For example, FR-NGHE-009.2 (protocol/codec selection) will need a DP that specifies the codec selection algorithm (e.g., bandwidth-aware, latency-aware, loss-aware heuristic) and the FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes). Revising DP-D at the next level will ensure that design decisions are traceable to specific sub-FRs and that trade-offs (e.g., FEC overhead vs. packet loss reduction) are explicitly analyzed.

assumptions: Customer network conditions are well-characterized and stable during operation.
FEC overhead (bandwidth increase) is acceptable within customer bandwidth budget.
Fallback to lower-bandwidth modes is operationally acceptable.
Redundant network paths (dual interfaces) are available on vehicle and operator console, or graceful degradation to single-path mode is acceptable.
Teleoperation module can be designed and tested independently of Vehicle and Safety Services modules (via mock interfaces).
Packet loss is the primary quality-of-service metric; latency and jitter are secondary (to be addressed in separate FRs).
open_risks: Network degradation risk: Unexpected network conditions (interference, congestion, topology changes) could exceed packet loss threshold. Mitigation: real-time monitoring and operator alerts; fallback modes.
Operator acceptance risk: Fallback to lower-bandwidth modes may be rejected by operators. Mitigation: clear operational procedures, training, and user studies.

Integration risk: Teleoperation module must interface cleanly with Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link. Misaligned interface contracts could cause integration failures. Mitigation: explicit interface specifications and integration testing.
Safety criticality risk: If Teleoperation module fails, safety alerts must still be generated. Mitigation: fail-safe SafetyAlert channel independent of main teleoperation data path.

Scalability risk: DP-D (FEC, codec switching, path diversity) may not scale to very high-bandwidth scenarios (e.g., 4K video + multiple sensor streams). Mitigation: performance testing and optimization at next decomposition level.
evidence_needed: Network characterization data: packet loss, latency, jitter, bandwidth profiles for customer deployment scenarios (WiFi, cellular, hybrid).
Codec performance data: bitrate, latency, quality metrics for candidate codecs (H.264, H.265, VP9, AV1) under various network conditions.
FEC performance data: overhead, latency, effectiveness for candidate FEC schemes (Reed-Solomon, Turbo, LDPC) under various packet loss rates.
Operator feedback: usability studies on fallback modes, alert mechanisms, and real-time monitoring dashboards.
Integration test results: end-to-end packet loss measurements with Vehicle and Safety Services modules under simulated and live network conditions.
Safety analysis: FMEA or fault tree analysis for Teleoperation module to identify failure modes and mitigation strategies.
